topics.title: Java I/O & NIO
topics.description: Java provides two generations of I/O APIs: the classic java.io package (streams, readers, writers) and the modern java.nio package (channels, buffers, NIO.2 Path/Files API). Classic I/O is stream-based and blocking. NIO is buffer-oriented and can be non-blocking. NIO.2 (Java 7+) adds a completely redesigned file-system API (java.nio.file) that replaces the old java.io.File with Path and Files — more reliable, more expressive, and fully integrated with try-with-resources.
topics.story: Think of classic I/O like a garden hose — water (data) flows one byte at a time through the hose. You can add a bigger nozzle (BufferedReader) to process bigger chunks, but you still deal with a continuous stream. NIO is like a water truck with a tank (ByteBuffer) — you fill the tank, drive it to the destination, and empty it all at once. NIO.2 (Path/Files) is the GPS and logistics system for the truck — it tells you where files are, watches for changes, and handles the filesystem itself, not just the data flow.
topics.analogy: Classic I/O = reading a book one letter at a time (InputStream) vs one line at a time (BufferedReader). NIO.2 Path/Files = the library card system — Path is the call number, Files is the librarian who fetches the book, moves it, or watches for new arrivals (WatchService). ByteBuffer = a whiteboard where you write data, erase, and reuse — not a scroll you read once and discard like an InputStream.
topics.memoryAnchor: I/O HIERARCHY: InputStream/OutputStream (bytes) → Reader/Writer (chars) → Buffered wrappers (performance). NIO.2: Path (address) + Files (operations) + WatchService (events). ALWAYS use try-with-resources for any I/O resource. BufferedReader.lines() returns Stream<String> — lazy, perfect for large files. Serialization: implements Serializable, declare serialVersionUID, mark sensitive fields transient.
topics.firstPrinciples: Every I/O operation crosses the OS kernel boundary — expensive. Buffering batches multiple small reads/writes into fewer large OS calls (100x faster for small operations). Classic I/O wraps streams in decorators (BufferedReader wraps FileReader). NIO uses Channels + ByteBuffers — the buffer is filled in one native call. Memory-mapped files (MappedByteBuffer) let the OS page-in file content directly into process memory — zero copy for reads.
topics.bruteForce: Without buffering: reading a 1MB file byte-by-byte makes 1,048,576 system calls. With BufferedReader (8KB buffer): 128 system calls. Without NIO.2: java.io.File.exists() on Windows has a known bug where it returns false for files with trailing spaces. Without try-with-resources: forgetting to close a FileInputStream leaks a file descriptor — limit is ~1024 per process on Linux.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use Files.readAllLines() or Files.lines() (lazy Stream) for text files. Use Files.copy() instead of manual stream copy loops. Use Files.writeString() for simple writes. Use BufferedReader/Writer for line-by-line processing. Use FileChannel + ByteBuffer for binary data and large files. Use MappedByteBuffer for read-heavy large files (search, indexing). Use WatchService for filesystem event monitoring instead of polling.
topics.whenToUse: Classic InputStream/OutputStream: binary data, wrapping around network sockets, legacy APIs. BufferedReader/Writer: line-by-line text processing, CSV, log parsing. Files.readAllLines(): small text files (<10MB) where you want all lines at once. Files.lines(): large files — lazy Stream, processes one line at a time without loading everything. FileChannel: large binary files, random access, file locking. MappedByteBuffer: read-heavy files >100MB where you access the same data multiple times. WatchService: monitoring config files, hot-reload, file drop zones.

examples.title: Classic I/O — Streams, Readers, Buffered Wrappers
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":11,"lineCode":"try (BufferedWriter bw = new BufferedWriter(new OutputStreamWriter(new FileOutputStream(filename), UTF_8)))","variables":{},"phase":"OPEN","annotation":"Decorator pattern: FileOutputStream (OS file handle) → OutputStreamWriter (byte-to-char encoding) → BufferedWriter (8192-char buffer). All three AutoCloseable — try-with-resources closes in reverse order."},{"line":13,"lineCode":"bw.write(\"Line 1: Hello Java I/O\");","variables":{"buffer":"Line 1..."},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Write goes into BufferedWriter's in-memory buffer — NO syscall yet. Buffer size is 8192 chars by default."},{"line":18,"lineCode":"// close() flushes buffer AND closes FileOutputStream","variables":{},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"Exiting try block: close() flushes remaining buffer contents to OS, then closes the file descriptor. Without BufferedWriter, each write() would make a syscall."},{"line":29,"lineCode":"while ((line = br.readLine()) != null)","variables":{"line":"Line 1: Hello Java I/O"},"phase":"LOOP_START","annotation":"readLine() reads up to newline from BufferedReader's buffer. Buffer is refilled from OS in 8KB chunks — many readLine() calls share one syscall."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    App[Application code] -->|write String| BW[BufferedWriter 8KB buffer]
    BW -->|flush when full| OSW[OutputStreamWriter charset encoding]
    OSW -->|encoded bytes| FOS[FileOutputStream syscall]
    FOS -->|write| Disk[(File on Disk)]
    Disk -->|read 8KB| FIS[FileInputStream]
    FIS -->|bytes| ISR[InputStreamReader decode]
    ISR -->|chars| BR[BufferedReader]
    BR -->|readLine| App2[Application code]
examples.pseudocode:
WRITE PIPELINE:
  application → BufferedWriter (buffer) → OutputStreamWriter (encode) → FileOutputStream (syscall)

READ PIPELINE:
  FileInputStream (syscall) → InputStreamReader (decode) → BufferedReader (buffer) → readLine()

KEY RULES:
  Always specify charset (UTF-8) — avoid platform default
  Always use try-with-resources — never forget to close
  BufferedReader.lines() returns lazy Stream<String>
  In.read(buffer) returns -1 at EOF — check this
examples.explanation: Classic I/O uses the Decorator pattern — each wrapper adds a capability layer. BufferedReader/Writer dramatically reduce syscall count (100x+ for small reads/writes). Always specify the charset explicitly (StandardCharsets.UTF_8) rather than relying on the platform default, which differs between Windows (Cp1252) and Linux (UTF-8). try-with-resources closes all decorators in reverse construction order, flushing buffers automatically.
examples.realWorldUse: Reading large log files line by line with BufferedReader.lines() — lazy Stream avoids loading the entire file. Writing CSV exports with BufferedWriter. Android uses InputStreamReader for reading assets. Spring's ResourceUtils reads classpath files via InputStream. Jackson's ObjectMapper accepts InputStream — always wrap in BufferedInputStream for performance.

examples.title: NIO.2 — Path, Files, WatchService
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":12,"lineCode":"Path home = Path.of(System.getProperty(\"user.home\"));","variables":{"home":"/home/user"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Path.of() creates an immutable path object — no I/O performed. Path is just an address; Files performs the actual operations."},{"line":25,"lineCode":"Files.writeString(tmpFile, \"Hello NIO.2...\");","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Files.writeString() is a convenience method that opens, writes, and closes the file atomically. Defaults to UTF-8 charset."},{"line":30,"lineCode":"try (Stream<String> lineStream = Files.lines(tmpFile)) {","variables":{},"phase":"OPEN","annotation":"Files.lines() returns a lazy Stream<String> backed by a BufferedReader. The stream MUST be closed (try-with-resources) to close the underlying file handle."},{"line":52,"lineCode":"tmpDir.register(watcher, ENTRY_CREATE, ENTRY_MODIFY, ENTRY_DELETE);","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Registers the directory with the OS-level file notification system (inotify on Linux, FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows). Zero polling — OS pushes events."},{"line":63,"lineCode":"key.reset();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"CRITICAL: must call key.reset() after processing events, otherwise WatchKey becomes CANCELLED and no further events are delivered for that directory."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[Path.of path address] --> B[Files operations]
    B --> C[Files.writeString write text]
    B --> D[Files.readAllLines all lines at once]
    B --> E[Files.lines lazy Stream]
    B --> F[Files.copy atomic copy]
    B --> G[Files.walk directory tree]
    H[WatchService] --> I[register directory]
    I --> J[OS sends events]
    J --> K[pollEvents]
    K --> L[key.reset]
    L --> J
examples.pseudocode:
PATH (immutable address, no I/O):
  Path.of(string)           create path
  path.resolve(other)       append segment
  path.getParent()          parent directory
  path.getFileName()        last component

FILES (actual I/O operations):
  Files.writeString(p, s)   write text
  Files.readAllLines(p)     all lines → List<String>
  Files.lines(p)            lazy Stream<String> — MUST close
  Files.copy(src, dst, opts)
  Files.walk(dir)           recursive stream of paths

WATCHSERVICE:
  register dir with kinds
  poll/take → WatchKey
  key.pollEvents() → List<WatchEvent>
  key.reset()  ← ALWAYS call after processing
examples.explanation: NIO.2 separates the address (Path) from the operation (Files). Path is immutable and lightweight — create many with no I/O cost. Files methods are static utility methods that handle resource management internally. Files.lines() returns a lazy Stream backed by a file handle — it MUST be closed. WatchService uses the OS native file-change notification (not polling), making it efficient even watching hundreds of files.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring DevTools uses WatchService to detect class changes and trigger hot-reload. Docker file drop zones use WatchService to process uploaded files. Files.walk() powers recursive delete, backup, and sync tools. Files.lines() is used in ETL pipelines to stream-process large CSV files without loading them into memory. IntelliJ IDEA uses NIO.2 WatchService to detect external file changes.

examples.title: Scanner & PrintWriter — CSV and Formatted Output
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":14,"lineCode":"try (PrintWriter pw = new PrintWriter(new BufferedWriter(new FileWriter(csvFile.toFile()))))","variables":{},"phase":"OPEN","annotation":"PrintWriter wraps BufferedWriter wraps FileWriter. PrintWriter adds printf/println convenience. BufferedWriter adds buffering. FileWriter opens the file."},{"line":24,"lineCode":"sc.useDelimiter(\",|\\n|\\r\\n\");","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Sets the Scanner's delimiter pattern. Default is any whitespace. This regex splits on comma OR Unix newline OR Windows newline — making Scanner a simple CSV tokenizer."},{"line":26,"lineCode":"String name = sc.next().trim();","variables":{"name":"Alice"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"sc.next() returns the next token according to the delimiter pattern — in this case the next comma-separated value."},{"line":47,"lineCode":"pw.printf(\"%-15s %5d %15s%n\", p.name(), p.age(), p.city());","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"printf format: %-15s = left-align in 15-char field. %5d = right-align integer in 5-char field. %n = platform newline. Produces a perfectly aligned table."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[PrintWriter printf] -->|formatted string| B[BufferedWriter]
    B -->|flush when full| C[FileWriter]
    C -->|bytes| D[(CSV file)]
    D -->|read| E[Scanner with delimiter]
    E -->|next nextInt nextDouble| F[typed tokens]
    F --> G[Person objects]
examples.pseudocode:
PRINTWRITER:
  printf(format, args)  — C-style formatted output
  println(s)            — write line with newline
  autoFlush=true        — flushes after every println/printf

SCANNER:
  new Scanner(file/string/inputstream)
  useDelimiter(regex)   — change token separator
  next()                — next String token
  nextInt()             — next int
  nextLine()            — entire line
  hasNext()             — check if more tokens

CSV PATTERN:
  delimiter = ",|\n|\r\n"  then next()/nextInt() per field
examples.explanation: Scanner is a flexible tokenizer that works with files, strings, and input streams. Setting a custom delimiter pattern makes it a simple CSV parser — each call to next()/nextInt() retrieves the next comma-separated field. PrintWriter adds printf-style formatting on top of any Writer or OutputStream. The %-15s format specifier creates left-aligned fixed-width columns, useful for human-readable reports and logs.
examples.realWorldUse: Reading configuration files with Scanner (key=value pairs, useDelimiter('='). PrintWriter for report generation in batch jobs. Scanner is the standard way to read console input in Java programs (new Scanner(System.in)). Spring Batch uses Reader/Writer patterns that mirror the BufferedReader/PrintWriter decorator stack. Log formatters use printf-style patterns via String.format or PrintWriter.printf.

examples.title: Object Serialization — Serializable, transient, serialVersionUID
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":10,"lineCode":"private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"serialVersionUID is the class's version stamp. If you change the class and don't update this, deserialization of old data throws InvalidClassException. Declare it explicitly — don't let the JVM generate it (JVM-generated ID changes with any class change)."},{"line":13,"lineCode":"private transient String password;","variables":{},"phase":"DECLARE","annotation":"transient fields are completely skipped during serialization — they are null/0/false after deserialization. Use for: sensitive data (passwords, keys), derived/cached values, thread-local state."},{"line":24,"lineCode":"oos.defaultWriteObject();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Writes all non-transient, non-static fields normally. Then we write additional custom data (roles.size()). This extends the default serialization format."},{"line":31,"lineCode":"ois.defaultReadObject();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Restores non-transient fields. Then reads the extra custom data we wrote. Finally recalculates the transient cachedHash from the restored fields."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[UserV1 object] -->|ObjectOutputStream| B[writeObject called]
    B -->|defaultWriteObject| C[username email roles serialized]
    B -->|transient fields| D[password cachedHash SKIPPED]
    C --> E[(Binary .bin file)]
    E -->|ObjectInputStream| F[readObject called]
    F -->|defaultReadObject| G[username email roles restored]
    F -->|transient fields| H[password=null cachedHash=0]
    G --> I[recalculate cachedHash in readObject]
    H --> I
examples.pseudocode:
SERIALIZATION:
  implements Serializable    mark class as serializable
  serialVersionUID = 1L      explicit version stamp
  transient field            skip this field

WRITE: new ObjectOutputStream(new BufferedOutputStream(fos))
  oos.writeObject(obj)       serialize object graph

READ: new ObjectInputStream(new BufferedInputStream(fis))
  (MyClass) ois.readObject() deserialize + cast

CUSTOM:
  writeObject(oos) { oos.defaultWriteObject(); write extras }
  readObject(ois)  { ois.defaultReadObject(); read extras; recalc transient }
examples.explanation: Serialization converts an object graph into bytes that can be saved or sent over the network. transient fields are excluded — use for passwords, cached values, and non-serializable fields. serialVersionUID acts as a schema version — if you add/remove fields and keep the same serialVersionUID, deserialization still works (new fields get default values). Custom writeObject/readObject let you extend or transform the format while preserving compatibility.
examples.realWorldUse: Java RMI (Remote Method Invocation) serializes method arguments over the network. HTTP session replication in Tomcat/JBoss uses serialization to replicate sessions across nodes. Spring Security's HttpSessionSecurityContextRepository serializes the SecurityContext — that's why Authentication objects must implement Serializable. Java caching (EHCache, Hazelcast) serializes objects to disk or network.

examples.title: NIO Channels & ByteBuffer — FileChannel and MappedByteBuffer
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":20,"lineCode":"ByteBuffer buf = ByteBuffer.allocate(1024);","variables":{"capacity":1024,"position":0,"limit":1024},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"ByteBuffer has capacity (max bytes), position (current read/write cursor), and limit (end of readable data). allocate() creates an on-heap buffer. allocateDirect() creates an off-heap buffer (faster for I/O)."},{"line":24,"lineCode":"buf.flip();","variables":{"position":"0","limit":"bytes_read"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"flip() prepares buffer for reading: sets limit = current position, sets position = 0. Without flip(), you'd read garbage beyond the actual data."},{"line":27,"lineCode":"buf.clear();","variables":{"position":0,"limit":1024},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"clear() prepares buffer for writing again: sets position = 0, limit = capacity. Data is not erased — just the pointers are reset."},{"line":36,"lineCode":"inCh.transferTo(0, inCh.size(), outCh);","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"transferTo() asks the OS to copy data directly from one file descriptor to another without copying through user space (sendfile syscall on Linux). Zero-copy — best performance for file copies."},{"line":48,"lineCode":"MappedByteBuffer mbb = fc.map(MapMode.READ_ONLY, 0, fc.size());","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"map() asks the OS to map the file into the process's virtual address space. No data is read into RAM immediately — pages are loaded on demand as you access positions. Random access is O(1) after mapping."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph ByteBuffer_states
      W[WRITE mode: pos=0 lim=capacity]
      W -->|read fills buffer| F[after read: pos=n lim=cap]
      F -->|flip| R[READ mode: pos=0 lim=n]
      R -->|write empties buffer| E[after write: pos=n lim=n]
      E -->|clear| W
    end
    subgraph MappedByteBuffer
      File[(File on Disk)] -->|mmap syscall| VM[Virtual Memory]
      VM -->|page fault on access| RAM[(RAM Page)]
      App[Application] -->|position access| VM
    end
examples.pseudocode:
BYTEBUFFER LIFECYCLE:
  allocate(n)  → write mode (pos=0, lim=n)
  channel.read(buf)  → fills buf, pos advances
  buf.flip()   → read mode (pos=0, lim=prev_pos)
  channel.write(buf) → drains buf, pos advances
  buf.clear()  → write mode again (pos=0, lim=cap)

ZERO-COPY:
  inChannel.transferTo(pos, count, outChannel)
  OS copies directly file→file, no user-space buffer

MEMORY-MAPPED:
  fc.map(READ_ONLY/READ_WRITE, offset, size)
  → MappedByteBuffer backed by virtual memory
  → Random access, OS handles paging
examples.explanation: FileChannel is the NIO counterpart to FileInputStream — it reads/writes into ByteBuffers instead of byte arrays. ByteBuffer has three key states controlled by position, limit, and capacity — flip() switches from write to read mode, clear() resets for writing. transferTo() is the highest-performance file copy — it bypasses user space entirely. MappedByteBuffer maps file contents into virtual memory — random access is O(1) and the OS caches hot pages automatically.
examples.realWorldUse: Kafka uses FileChannel.transferTo() for zero-copy sends from disk to network — this is a key reason Kafka is fast. Memory-mapped files are used by databases (H2, SQLite) for their data files — the OS page cache acts as a database buffer pool. Java's RandomAccessFile is superseded by FileChannel for binary file formats (ZIP, class files, Parquet). Lucene (Elasticsearch's search engine) uses MappedByteBuffer to index files.

problems.title: Read File to List
problems.description: Implement `readLines(String filePath)` that reads a text file and returns its contents as `List<String>`. Each element is one line. Empty files return an empty list. Use NIO.2 Files API.
problems.hint1: `Files.readAllLines(Path.of(filePath))` reads all lines with UTF-8 charset.
problems.hint2: Wrap in try-catch IOException and return empty list on error.
problems.hint3: For very large files use `Files.lines(path)` (lazy Stream) instead of readAllLines.
problems.constraints: Returns empty list on error or empty file.
problems.editorial: Files.readAllLines() reads the entire file into memory as a List<String>. Fine for small files (<10MB). For larger files use Files.lines() which returns a lazy Stream<String> and must be closed in a try-with-resources block. Both default to UTF-8 charset.

problems.title: Write List to File
problems.description: Implement `writeLines(String filePath, List<String> lines)` that writes each string on its own line to a file, overwriting if it exists. Use NIO.2.
problems.hint1: `Files.write(path, lines)` writes an Iterable<String> each on its own line.
problems.hint2: Or use `Files.writeString(path, String.join("\n", lines))`.
problems.hint3: To append instead of overwrite: add `StandardOpenOption.APPEND` as extra argument.
problems.constraints: Overwrites existing file.
problems.editorial: Files.write(path, Iterable<String>) writes each element followed by the system line separator. It is the NIO.2 replacement for a manual BufferedWriter loop. Always prefer this over FileWriter + loop for simple write operations.

problems.title: Count Lines and Words in a File
problems.description: Implement `fileStats(String filePath)` returning `int[]{lineCount, wordCount}`. Words are whitespace-separated tokens. Use `Files.lines()` stream for line count and split for word count.
problems.hint1: `Files.lines(path)` returns a lazy `Stream<String>` — close it with try-with-resources.
problems.hint2: Word count: split each line by `\\s+` and sum lengths. Skip empty strings from split.
problems.hint3: `.mapToLong(l -> l.trim().isEmpty() ? 0 : l.trim().split("\\s+").length).sum()`

testCases.input: Hello World Java\nNIO is fast\nThree words here

problems.constraints: Files.lines() must be closed. Empty lines count as lines but contribute 0 words.
problems.editorial: Files.lines() returns a lazy Stream — MUST be closed with try-with-resources to close the underlying file handle. Using a mutable stats array inside forEach is necessary because lambda can't modify local variables. An alternative is two separate stream passes (one for lines, one for words) but that opens the file twice.

problems.title: Copy File
problems.description: Implement `copyFile(String src, String dst)` that copies a file. If `dst` already exists, overwrite it. Use NIO.2 `Files.copy()`.
problems.hint1: `Files.copy(source, target, StandardCopyOption.REPLACE_EXISTING)`.
problems.hint2: Both `source` and `target` are `Path.of(string)`.
problems.hint3: To copy to a directory (not a file), use `target.resolve(source.getFileName())`.
problems.constraints: Overwrites destination if it exists.
problems.editorial: Files.copy() is atomic on most file systems — the destination either gets the complete copy or nothing. REPLACE_EXISTING silently overwrites. Without this option, Files.copy() throws FileAlreadyExistsException if the target exists. For large files across different file systems, Files.copy() falls back to a buffered stream copy internally.

problems.title: File Exists and Size Check
problems.description: Implement `fileInfo(String path)` returning a `Map<String, Object>` with keys: `exists` (boolean), `size` (long, bytes), `isDirectory` (boolean), `readable` (boolean). Use NIO.2.
problems.hint1: `Files.exists(path)`, `Files.size(path)`, `Files.isDirectory(path)`, `Files.isReadable(path)`.
problems.hint2: If file does not exist, size should be 0 and other flags false.
problems.hint3: `Path.of(pathString)` creates the Path object.
problems.constraints: No exception thrown for non-existent path.
problems.editorial: Files.exists() is a safe boolean check — never throws. Files.size() throws IOException for non-existent files, so guard it. Note: java.io.File.exists() has a known bug on Windows with trailing spaces; Files.exists() does not. All these methods follow symlinks by default — pass LinkOption.NOFOLLOW_LINKS to check the symlink itself.

problems.title: Read a CSV Row
problems.description: Implement `parseCsvLine(String line)` that splits a simple CSV line (no quoted fields with commas inside) into a `List<String>`. Trim whitespace from each field. Handle empty fields.
problems.hint1: `line.split(",", -1)` — the -1 keeps trailing empty strings.
problems.hint2: Stream the array, trim each value, collect to list.
problems.hint3: For `"Alice, Bob, ,Carol"` the result should be `[Alice, Bob, , Carol]` (empty string at index 2).
problems.constraints: No quoted fields containing commas. Handles empty fields.
problems.editorial: split(",", -1) is critical — without the -1 limit, trailing empty strings are discarded. This means a line ending in a comma would lose the last empty field. stream + map(trim) handles spaces around commas. For production CSV (quoted fields, escaped commas), use Apache Commons CSV or OpenCSV.

problems.title: Recursive Directory Walker
problems.description: Implement `listFilesRecursive(String dirPath, String extension)` that returns a sorted `List<String>` of all file paths (absolute) under the directory that match the given extension (e.g. `.java`, `.txt`). Use `Files.walk()`.
problems.hint1: `Files.walk(Path.of(dir))` returns a `Stream<Path>` of all entries recursively.
problems.hint2: Filter: `Files.isRegularFile(p) && p.toString().endsWith(extension)`.
problems.hint3: Collect to list, sort, return. Close the stream with try-with-resources.

testCases.input: dir with a.txt, b.txt, sub/c.txt, d.java, filter .txt

problems.constraints: Must close Stream from Files.walk(). Sort alphabetically.
problems.editorial: Files.walk() returns a Stream<Path> that must be closed — it holds open file handles for the directory iteration. Filter chain: isRegularFile excludes directories and symlinks; endsWith filters by extension. Sorting the string paths gives alphabetical order. For glob patterns use Files.newDirectoryStream(dir, '**.txt') instead.

problems.title: File Change Watcher
problems.description: Implement `watchForChanges(String dirPath, long timeoutMs)` that monitors a directory for file creation events for up to `timeoutMs` milliseconds. Returns a `List<String>` of filenames that were created during the watch period. Use `WatchService`.
problems.hint1: `FileSystems.getDefault().newWatchService()` — register dir with `ENTRY_CREATE`.
problems.hint2: `watcher.poll(timeoutMs, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)` — returns null on timeout.
problems.hint3: After processing events: `key.reset()` — required to continue receiving events.
problems.constraints: key.reset() is mandatory. Close WatchService in try-with-resources.
problems.editorial: WatchService uses OS-native notifications (inotify/FSEvents) — no polling. The loop uses a deadline to respect the timeout while still processing multiple events. key.reset() transitions the key back to READY state — without it, the key stays in SIGNALLED and no further events are delivered. event.context() returns the filename relative to the watched directory, not the full path.

problems.title: CSV Parser With Quoted Fields
problems.description: Implement `parseCsvLine(String line)` that correctly handles quoted fields containing commas. For example `Alice,"New York, NY",30` should return `[Alice, New York, NY, 30]`. Quotes themselves are not returned.
problems.hint1: Use a state machine: track whether you're inside quotes or not.
problems.hint2: When inside quotes, commas are part of the field. When outside quotes, commas are delimiters.
problems.hint3: Character-by-character scan: toggle `inQuotes` on `'"'`, add to current field otherwise.
problems.constraints: Double-quote escape (`""`) not required. Single-level quoting only.
problems.editorial: State machine parsing: one boolean `inQuotes` tracks whether the current character is inside a quoted field. When `inQuotes=true`, commas are treated as regular characters. This is the core of RFC 4180 CSV parsing. For production use Apache Commons CSV — it also handles escaped quotes (`""` within a quoted field) and multiline fields.

problems.title: Merge Sorted Files
problems.description: Given two sorted text files (one integer per line, ascending), merge them into a single sorted output file using an efficient merge (k-way merge pattern, not load-all-then-sort). Read both files line by line.
problems.hint1: Open both files with BufferedReader. Read the first line from each as the 'current' value.
problems.hint2: Compare current values. Write the smaller one, advance that reader.
problems.hint3: When one reader is exhausted, copy remaining lines from the other.
problems.constraints: O(1) memory — do not load both files into memory.
problems.editorial: Classic 2-way merge from merge sort. Read one line from each file as the 'current' pair. Write the smaller, advance that reader. This is O(n+m) time, O(1) memory beyond the line buffers. This is the basis of external merge sort used in databases and MapReduce for sorting datasets larger than RAM.

problems.title: File-Based LRU Cache
problems.description: Implement `FileLRUCache` that stores key-value pairs as files in a temp directory. Constructor takes capacity. `put(key, value)` writes value to a file named by key, evicting the LRU entry if at capacity. `get(key)` reads and returns the file content, or null if not found.
problems.hint1: Use `LinkedHashMap(capacity, 0.75f, true)` with `removeEldestEntry` override for LRU ordering.
problems.hint2: `put`: write `Files.writeString(dir.resolve(key), value)`. On eviction in `removeEldestEntry`, delete the evicted file.
problems.hint3: `get`: check the map (for LRU ordering), then `Files.readString(dir.resolve(key))`.

testCases.input: capacity=3, put a,b,c; get a; put d; get b

problems.constraints: LinkedHashMap access-order=true for LRU semantics.
problems.editorial: LinkedHashMap(cap, 0.75f, true) maintains access order — get() moves elements to the tail. removeEldestEntry returns true when size exceeds capacity, triggering automatic removal of the head (LRU). The file operation mirrors the map operation. This combines an in-memory index with persistent file storage.

problems.title: Buffered Word Frequency Counter
problems.description: Implement `wordFrequency(String filePath)` that reads a large text file line by line using `BufferedReader` (not Files.readAllLines which loads everything) and returns a `Map<String, Integer>` of word counts. Words are lowercase, letters only.
problems.hint1: Open with `Files.newBufferedReader(Path.of(filePath))`.
problems.hint2: Per line: `line.toLowerCase().split("[^a-z]+")` splits on any non-letter character.
problems.hint3: `map.merge(word, 1, Integer::sum)` increments count or initialises to 1.

testCases.input: the cat sat on the mat\nthe cat ate the rat

problems.constraints: O(1) memory per line — no loading entire file. O(V) memory for the frequency map.
problems.editorial: BufferedReader.readLine() reads one line at a time using an 8KB OS-level buffer. map.merge(key, 1, Integer::sum) is the idiomatic Java 8+ way to increment a count: if key absent, sets value to 1; if present, applies Integer::sum to current value and 1. This streams through files of any size using constant I/O memory.

problems.title: tail -f — Live File Tailer
problems.description: Implement `tailFile(String filePath, int lastNLines)` that reads the last N lines of a file (like `tail -n N`). Then implement `tailFollow(String filePath, Consumer<String> onLine, long durationMs)` that watches the file for new appended lines for `durationMs` ms and calls `onLine` for each new line (like `tail -f`).
problems.hint1: For tail -n: use a `Deque<String>` of fixed size N. Read all lines, keep last N.
problems.hint2: For tail -f: track file position with `Files.size()`. On ENTRY_MODIFY event, read from last position to current end.
problems.hint3: Use a `RandomAccessFile` or `FileChannel` positioned at the last-read byte to read only new content.
problems.constraints: tailFollow must use WatchService — no polling. tail reads last N without loading whole file into memory.
problems.editorial: tail -n: a circular Deque of size N is the classic solution — add to end, remove from front when full. tail -f: track file position (byte offset), watch for MODIFY events, seek to last position and read new content. RandomAccessFile.seek() enables reading from any byte position. The charset conversion handles RandomAccessFile's ISO-8859-1 byte reading.

problems.title: Binary File Diff
problems.description: Implement `binaryDiff(String file1, String file2)` that compares two binary files and returns a `List<String>` describing the differences. Each diff entry: `'offset 0x{hex}: file1={hex} file2={hex}'`. If files have different lengths, note that too.
problems.hint1: Open both files with `FileChannel`. Read chunks into two ByteBuffers of equal size.
problems.hint2: Compare byte-by-byte within each chunk. Track absolute offset = chunkStart + byteIndex.
problems.hint3: If files have different sizes, append `'file1 size=X, file2 size=Y'` as first diff entry.

testCases.input: Hello vs Help+o (byte 0x6C vs 0x70 at offset 3)

problems.constraints: Chunk-based reading — do not load both files into memory.
problems.editorial: FileChannel reads into ByteBuffer in 4KB chunks — efficient for any file size. The offset is tracked across chunks. v & 0xFF converts signed byte (-128..127) to unsigned (0..255) for hex display. This is the core of the `diff --binary` command. For large files the chunk size can be tuned (larger = fewer syscalls, more memory).

problems.title: Memory-Mapped Large File Search
problems.description: Implement `searchInFile(String filePath, String searchTerm)` that returns the byte offset of the first occurrence of `searchTerm` (UTF-8) in a large file using `MappedByteBuffer`. Return -1 if not found.
problems.hint1: Map the file: `fc.map(MapMode.READ_ONLY, 0, fc.size())` gives direct memory access.
problems.hint2: Decode to String: `StandardCharsets.UTF_8.decode(mbb).toString()` — only do this for moderate-size files.
problems.hint3: For byte-level search: scan mbb byte by byte comparing against the search bytes.
problems.constraints: Use MappedByteBuffer — no reading into a byte[] for the whole file.
problems.editorial: MappedByteBuffer.get(i) accesses file bytes at position i directly from virtual memory — the OS loads the relevant page on demand. The outer label on the loop enables continue outer to skip to the next starting position on mismatch. For production use, Boyer-Moore-Horspool or KMP would be O(n) instead of O(n*m). MappedByteBuffer is ideal here because the OS caches hot pages across repeated searches.

problems.title: Concurrent File Processor
problems.description: Implement `processFilesAsync(List<Path> files, Function<Path, String> processor)` that processes multiple files concurrently using `CompletableFuture`. Each file is processed by the given function. Returns `Map<Path, String>` of results. Preserve all results even if some files fail (use exceptionally to return error message).
problems.hint1: `CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> processor.apply(path))` for each file.
problems.hint2: Chain `.exceptionally(e -> "ERROR: " + e.getMessage())` to handle failures gracefully.
problems.hint3: Collect all futures, then `CompletableFuture.allOf(futures).join()` to wait, then get() each.

testCases.output: CONTENT OF FILE 1 ... CONTENT OF FILE 5

problems.constraints: All results collected even if some fail. Uses common ForkJoinPool.
problems.editorial: supplyAsync uses ForkJoinPool.commonPool() by default — parallel for CPU-bound work. For I/O-bound work (reading files), pass a custom Executor with more threads (Executors.newFixedThreadPool(20)). exceptionally catches CompletionException and provides a fallback value — ensures all futures complete. allOf().join() waits for all to finish, then individual join() calls retrieve results.

problems.title: Custom Serialization With Versioning
problems.description: Implement a `ConfigV2` class with new field `theme` added after `ConfigV1(username, fontSize)`. Demonstrate backwards compatibility: deserializing a V1 byte stream into ConfigV2 should succeed with `theme` defaulting to `'dark'`. Use `serialVersionUID` and `readObject` with `defaultReadObject`.
problems.hint1: Keep `serialVersionUID = 1L` in V2 — same as V1. This tells Java the classes are compatible.
problems.hint2: In V2's readObject: call `ois.defaultReadObject()` which restores only fields present in the stream. New fields (theme) stay at their initialized default.
problems.hint3: Initialize `theme = 'dark'` as a field initializer — defaultReadObject restores V1 fields, leaving theme at its default.
problems.constraints: Same serialVersionUID = backwards compatible. Different UID = InvalidClassException.
problems.editorial: Java serialization compares the stream's serialVersionUID with the class's serialVersionUID. If they match, deserialization proceeds — missing fields in the stream get default values (null, 0, false). defaultReadObject only restores fields that are present in the stream. New fields added to V2 are not in the stream, so they keep their field initializer value ('dark'). This is the standard approach for serialization version evolution.

problems.title: Append-Only Log File
problems.description: Implement `AppendLog` with `append(String message)` that writes a timestamped log entry, and `readAll()` that returns all entries. Entries format: `[ISO-timestamp] message`. File is created on first append. Entries survive JVM restarts (persistent). Use `StandardOpenOption.APPEND`.
problems.hint1: `Files.writeString(path, entry, StandardOpenOption.CREATE, StandardOpenOption.APPEND)`.
problems.hint2: Timestamp: `java.time.Instant.now().toString()` for ISO-8601 format.
problems.hint3: `readAll()`: use `Files.readAllLines(path)` — each line is one log entry.
problems.constraints: StandardOpenOption.APPEND never overwrites existing content.
problems.editorial: StandardOpenOption.CREATE creates the file if absent; APPEND positions the write pointer at the end. This is the append-only log pattern used by databases (WAL — Write-Ahead Log), Kafka (commit log), and Redis (AOF). The key property: once written, entries are never modified — only new entries are added. This makes the log crash-safe: a crash mid-write at worst adds a partial last line, which can be detected and truncated on recovery.

problems.title: ZIP File Reader and Writer
problems.description: Implement `createZip(Map<String, String> entries, String zipPath)` that creates a ZIP file where each key is the filename inside the ZIP and the value is its text content. Implement `readZip(String zipPath)` that returns the same `Map<String, String>` by reading back all entries.
problems.hint1: `ZipOutputStream(new BufferedOutputStream(new FileOutputStream(zipPath)))` — wrap with BufferedOutputStream.
problems.hint2: Per entry: `zos.putNextEntry(new ZipEntry(filename))` then write bytes then `zos.closeEntry()`.
problems.hint3: Read: `ZipInputStream(new BufferedInputStream(new FileInputStream(zipPath)))` — `zis.getNextEntry()` returns null at end.
problems.constraints: UTF-8 encoding for all text content.
problems.editorial: ZipOutputStream is a FilterOutputStream — it sits on top of any OutputStream. putNextEntry() starts a new entry; closeEntry() finalises it; close() on ZipOutputStream finalises the ZIP directory at the end. ZipInputStream.getNextEntry() returns null when all entries are read. readAllBytes() (Java 9+) reads the entire entry content in one call.

problems.title: NIO Non-Blocking File Server Skeleton
problems.description: Implement a simple NIO-based file serving skeleton: `FileServer` that accepts connections on a `ServerSocketChannel` (non-blocking), reads a filename from the client, and sends back the file's content bytes. Use `Selector` to handle multiple connections without threads.
problems.hint1: `ServerSocketChannel.open()` → `configureBlocking(false)` → `bind(port)` → register with `Selector` for `OP_ACCEPT`.
problems.hint2: On OP_ACCEPT: `ssc.accept()` gives a `SocketChannel`. Configure non-blocking, register for `OP_READ`.
problems.hint3: On OP_READ: read the filename, then `FileChannel.open(path).transferTo(0, size, clientChannel)` to send the file.
problems.constraints: Single thread handles all connections via Selector. No blocking I/O on the event loop.
problems.editorial: The Reactor pattern: one thread multiplexes many connections via Selector. OP_ACCEPT fires when a new client connects. OP_READ fires when data arrives. The key insight is configureBlocking(false) — without it, accept() and read() would block the entire event loop. selector.selectedKeys().remove() is mandatory — not removing a key causes it to fire again immediately. This is the foundation of Netty, Tomcat NIO connector, and Node.js (which uses libuv, the C equivalent).
